title: Why the Founding Fathers did not give the president the power to impose tariffs
gist: A trade-law scholar argues the Constitution gives Congress, not the president, exclusive tariff authority, and walks through how courts have now twice struck down Trump's broadest tariff moves (IEEPA's 'reciprocal tariff' and a Section 122 balance-of-payments tariff), leaving only narrow, sector-specific tools like Section 232 and Section 301 as his remaining (legally shakier) levers.

insights.claim: There is no inherent presidential tariff power in the US system; tariff authority belongs solely to Congress, and the president only has whatever narrow, explicitly limited tariff powers Congress chooses to grant.
insights.speaker: Alan Wm. Wolff
insights.so_what: Any presidential tariff action not tied to a specific, narrow statutory grant is legally vulnerable to court challenge — a recurring pattern investors should expect to continue.
insights.quote: There is no inherent presidential tariff power. That is solely the prerogative of the Congress.

insights.claim: The Founders' decision to vest tariff and tax power exclusively in Congress traces to English history: King Charles I lost the right to impose taxes (including tariffs) in 1628, which required a civil war and ended in his execution — an event as historically close to the Founders as World War II is to people today.
insights.speaker: Alan Wm. Wolff
insights.so_what: Explains why US courts treat tariff-by-executive-fiat as a near-sacred constitutional violation rather than a technicality, making judicial pushback on unilateral tariffs structurally likely, not a one-off.
insights.locator: The Founding Fathers intentionally did not give the president tariff authority

insights.claim: Supreme Court Justice Neil Gorsuch, in a concurring opinion striking down the president's use of IEEPA for the 'reciprocal' tariffs, argued the deliberative, multi-representative nature of the legislative process is precisely why tariff power belongs to Congress rather than a single official.
insights.speaker: Alan Wm. Wolff
insights.so_what: Signals the current Supreme Court's reasoning is philosophically opposed to unilateral executive tariff-setting, a durable headwind for future emergency-authority tariff attempts.
insights.quote: the deliberative nature of the legislative process was the whole point of its design. Through that process, the Nation can tap the combined wisdom of the people's elected representatives, not just that of one faction or man. There, deliberation tempers impulse, and compromise hammers disagreements into workable solutions.
insights.locator: The Founding Fathers intentionally did not give the president tariff authority

insights.claim: The author concludes that having now failed twice to impose comprehensive tariffs without Congress, Trump has no broad tariff authority under either the Constitution or any statute Congress has passed, and further workarounds (including Section 301) will also fail.
insights.speaker: Alan Wm. Wolff
insights.so_what: An explicit forecast that any further attempt at economy-wide tariffs will lose in court, useful for handicapping legal/political risk around future blanket tariff announcements.
insights.quote: President Trump has now attempted twice to impose comprehensive tariffs without resort to Congress . Use of Section 301 or a claim of some other device will also not succeed.

insights.claim: Trump's 'reciprocal tariff,' declared April 2, 2025 and put into effect around August 2025 under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act (IEEPA), lasted only about six months before the Supreme Court ruled on February 20, 2026 that sanctions authority under IEEPA is not tariff authority at all.
insights.speaker: Alan Wm. Wolff
insights.so_what: Confirms the 'reciprocal tariff' regime is dead as of Feb 2026 — any pricing, hedging or sourcing decisions still assuming that tariff schedule are stale.
insights.quote: The Supreme Court held on February 20, 2026, that sanctions authority was not tariff authority at all.

insights.claim: The author personally believed the Supreme Court's IEEPA ruling went too far, arguing there could be circumstances where a tariff functions legitimately as a sanction to regulate trade — but that is not what the Court decided.
insights.speaker: Alan Wm. Wolff
insights.so_what: A trade-law insider flags the ruling as broader than necessary, hinting at possible future narrowing or litigation over tariff-as-sanction use cases.

insights.claim: After the IEEPA tariff was struck down, Trump replaced it with a claim of a balance-of-payments problem under Section 122 of the Trade Act of 1974, announcing a 10-15 percent tariff that settled at 10 percent — but the Court of International Trade quickly rejected this too, finding no actual balance-of-payments problem existed to trigger the 150-day emergency authority.
insights.speaker: Alan Wm. Wolff
insights.so_what: A second blanket-tariff attempt was also invalidated within the same window, reinforcing that broad, non-sector-specific tariffs currently have no durable legal basis in the US courts.

themes.theme: Remaining tariff tools and sector watchlist

insights.claim: Trump has no authority to raise tariffs via trade agreements because President Biden let Trade Promotion Authority (TPA) lapse completely in 2021, and Congress had already removed tariff-increasing authority from TPA in the 1988 Omnibus Trade and Competitiveness Act — meaning deals like those with the EU and UK could not be backed by a unilateral tariff increase under that route.
insights.speaker: Alan Wm. Wolff
insights.so_what: Trade-agreement-linked tariffs (EU, UK deals) rest on legally weaker ground than assumed, since the traditional TPA tariff-raising mechanism has been unavailable since 1988/2021.

insights.claim: Under Section 232 of the Trade Expansion Act of 1962 (national security tariffs), Trump has imposed tariffs, following Commerce Department investigation and Defense Secretary input, on steel, aluminum, copper, automobiles and parts, trucks and buses, lumber and wood products, semiconductors, and processed critical minerals — with pharmaceuticals, commercial aircraft and jet engines, drones, wind turbines, medical equipment/devices, PPE, and robotics/industrial automation among the products under active investigation for potential future tariffs.
insights.speaker: Alan Wm. Wolff
insights.so_what: A concrete forward watchlist: pharma, aircraft/jet engines, drones, wind turbines, medical devices, PPE, and robotics/automation are the named sectors most exposed to a near-term Section 232 tariff decision.

insights.claim: With IEEPA and Section 122 both struck down, USTR has turned to Section 301 trade retaliatory authority, finding 60 countries guilty of not enforcing adequate forced-labor laws and investigating 16 additional countries for maintaining structural excess capacity, as a substitute mechanism for the lost broad tariff power.
insights.speaker: Alan Wm. Wolff
insights.so_what: Names the specific legal vehicle (Section 301, forced-labor and excess-capacity findings) now driving tariff risk on a widening set of trading partners — worth tracking which of the 60+16 countries face actual tariff action.

insights.claim: The author argues Section 301 cannot legally substitute for comprehensive tariff power because it is a selective, country-by-country authority never designed to be imposed on all countries at once, and there is no legislative history suggesting it amounts to a transfer of Congress's full tariff authority to the president — nor can Congress constitutionally delegate all its tariff authority to the president in the first place.
insights.speaker: Alan Wm. Wolff
insights.so_what: Sets up an expectation that Section 301 tariffs, if applied broadly/simultaneously across many countries, are themselves vulnerable to the same court challenges that killed IEEPA and Section 122.

insights.claim: The 1890 McKinley Tariff is misattributed to presidential action: William McKinley was chairman of the House Ways and Means Committee at the time, but the sitting president who signed the bill was Benjamin Harrison — it was purely a Congressional measure.
insights.speaker: Alan Wm. Wolff
insights.so_what: Debunks a common historical shorthand used to argue presidents have long driven tariff policy, reinforcing the article's core thesis that tariffs have always been a Congressional act.

insights.claim: Nixon's 1971 emergency 10 percent import surcharge during a balance-of-payments crisis was not actually authorized by the wartime Trading with the Enemy Act his lawyers claimed to invoke; Congress, mistakenly believing he had used that authority, repealed it — and Nixon's subsequent 1973 Trade Reform Act proposal to unilaterally rewrite US law to remove nontariff barriers was rejected by the Senate.
insights.speaker: Alan Wm. Wolff
insights.so_what: Shows a direct historical precedent for courts/Congress rejecting presidential claims to sweeping emergency tariff power — the same playbook now repeating with IEEPA and Section 122.

insights.claim: After Nixon's unilateral-authority proposal was rejected, Congress instead created the 'fast track' up-or-down vote procedure for trade agreements — later formalized as Trade Promotion Authority — requiring the president to secure a new statute for each grant rather than a standing tariff power.
insights.speaker: Alan Wm. Wolff
insights.so_what: Explains the institutional origin of Trade Promotion Authority, the mechanism whose 2021 lapse (per Biden) now blocks Trump from tariff increases tied to trade agreements.
